coredata.dc:title: Integration of PEG and PEI with graphene quantum dots to fabricate pH-responsive nanostars for colon cancer suppression in vitro and in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Guang-Yu
Lo, Pei-Ying
Cho, Er-Chieh
Zheng, Jia-Huei
Li, Min
Huang, Jen-Hsien
Lee, Kuen-Chan
coredata.dc:description: There has been great improvement in nanomaterial fields, and the biomedical potential of nanomaterials as drug delivery system is under intensive studies. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered to be the next generation of carbon-based nanomaterial for biomedical applications. In this study, we utilized green fluorescent protein (GFP) nucleic acid, DNA-targeting chemoreagent doxorubicin (DOX), and branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) conjugated GQDs, to form pH-responsive nanostar drug carrier for colon cancer suppression investigation. GFP expression plasmid was applied to examine the delivery capacity of our drug carrier. DOX was encapsulated by intrinsic π-π interaction to form GQDs-polymer-DOX conjugates (GECD) as drug carrier. We proposed that once GECD enters the tumor lesion, the acidic tumor microenvironment protonated the tertiary amine of GECD from neutral to mild positive, possessed higher affinity to negatively charged cell membrane, triggered the drug release, and then cancer cells would be inhibited. The anti-cancer ability of our drug carrier system, GECD, was demonstrated in cancer cells by cell proliferation assay. Moreover, GECD exhibited its powerful anti-tumor capacity in the mice xenograft model. The in vivo and in vitro results indicate that our GECD with high cancer suppression capacity could be adopted for future cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
GFP nucleic acid
Targeted delivery
Drug delivery system
Tumor suppression

Cell uptake evaluation of NPs in a transfection test
Discussion about a possible cell-entry mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hualien Tzu-Chi Hospital Joint Cancer Center Grant-Focus on Colon Cancer Research

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was partly supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST 109-2320-B-038-038, MOST 110-2314-B-152-001 and MOST 110-2320-B-038-027), Taiwan, and WanFang Hospital, Chi-Mei Medical Center, and Hualien Tzu-Chi Hospital Joint Cancer Center Grant-Focus on Colon Cancer Research (MOHW110-TDU-B-212-144020, funded by Health and welfare surcharge of tobacco products), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University and the CPC Corporation, Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

This project was partly supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST 109-2320-B-038-038, MOST 110-2314-B-152-001 and MOST 110-2320-B-038-027), Taiwan, and WanFang Hospital, Chi-Mei Medical Center, and Hualien Tzu-Chi Hospital Joint Cancer Center Grant-Focus on Colon Cancer Research (MOHW110-TDU-B-212-144020, funded by Health and welfare surcharge of tobacco products), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University and the CPC Corporation, Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The high-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectra of (a) C 1 s and (b) N 1 s.
(a) zeta potential of GQDs, GE, GEC, GECD, and GECD at pH = 5.5; (b) PL spectra of GQDs, GE and GEC.
The (a) TEM and (b) HR-TEM images of GEC; size distribution and the UV–Vis spectra of GQDs, GE, and GECD.
The in vitro drug release behavior of GECD within pH 5.5 and pH 7.4 PBS buffer for 96 hr.
The transfection images of HCT116 cells with GEC treatment or not under the photoluminescence microscopy.
Cell viability of HCT116 cells at 24 hr, 48 hr, and 72 hr.

Tumor suppression examination of GECD in mice xenograft model. Xenograft experiment was performed with DOX or GECD treatment as in the materials and methods. The average sizes of the tumors in each group were examined (a), and the tumor inhibition rate was analyzed at the end of the experiment (b). (c) The body weight of the mice in each group were monitored along the experiment. (d) The representative pictures of tumors in each group was presented. HCT116 stands for the group without treatments. DOX stands for doxorubicin treatment group, and GECD stands for GECD nanoparticle treatment group. *P < .05.
The synthetic process of GE, GEC, and GECD.
The proposed drug release mechanism after entering the tumor cell.

head.ce:title: Integration of PEG and PEI with graphene quantum dots to fabricate pH-responsive nanostars for colon cancer suppression in vitro and in vivo

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Science Education, National Taipei University of Education, No.134, Sec. 2, Heping E. Rd., Da-an District, Taipei City 106, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The proposed drug release mechanism after entering the tumor cell.

There has been great improvement in nanomaterial fields, and the biomedical potential of nanomaterials as drug delivery system is under intensive studies. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered to be the next generation of carbon-based nanomaterial for biomedical applications. In this study, we utilized green fluorescent protein (GFP) nucleic acid, DNA-targeting chemoreagent doxorubicin (DOX), and branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) conjugated GQDs, to form pH-responsive nanostar drug carrier for colon cancer suppression investigation. GFP expression plasmid was applied to examine the delivery capacity of our drug carrier. DOX was encapsulated by intrinsic π-π interaction to form GQDs-polymer-DOX conjugates (GECD) as drug carrier. We proposed that once GECD enters the tumor lesion, the acidic tumor microenvironment protonated the tertiary amine of GECD from neutral to mild positive, possessed higher affinity to negatively charged cell membrane, triggered the drug release, and then cancer cells would be inhibited. The anti-cancer ability of our drug carrier system, GECD, was demonstrated in cancer cells by cell proliferation assay. Moreover, GECD exhibited its powerful anti-tumor capacity in the mice xenograft model. The in vivo and in vitro results indicate that our GECD with high cancer suppression capacity could be adopted for future cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: With recent advances in nanotechnology, nanomedicine has endowed modern medicine with more-specific and -efficient therapies toward cancer or other diseases [1,2]. Nanoparticle (NP)-based drug delivery provides enhanced pharmacokinetics [3], specific therapeutics released in the tumor area [4], alleviation of side effects, and the ability to circumvent biological barriers [5].

Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with a lateral size of < 10 nm and superior biocompatibility, are considered to be the next generation of carbon-based nanomaterials for numerous biomedical applications, including biosensing, antioxidation, and drug/gene delivery [6-9]. Recent studies reported that abundant pi-electrons within GQDs provide a platform for encapsulating hydrophobic anticancer drugs and other cargos through π-π interactions [10]. Additionally, with top-down exfoliation, the edges of oxidized GQDs typically contain a variety of functional moieties, such as carboxylate, carbonyl, and epoxy groups, which favor the attachment of proteins and polymers via facile cross-linking techniques without compromising their unique structures [11]. Physicochemical properties of GQD-based nanomedicines therefore can be easily tuned by decorating them with functional counterparts to impart several advantages such as lower systemic toxicity, enhanced drug efficacy, and precise cancer targeting [12-15]. Although these riveting properties make GQDs ideal candidates as drug carriers, the delivery of NP-based therapeutics to target tissues still suffers from rapid degradation and clearance from the bloodstream. In this respect, PEGylation, the attachment of polyethylene glycol (PEG) onto macrostructures, has been widely adopted to prolong the circulation time of therapeutics in the bloodstream and avoid rapid uptake by macrophages of the mononuclear phagocyte system (MPS), both of which vastly increase the biocompatibility of the nanocarriers [16]. Ko et al. reported a disulfide linkage GQD-PEG nanoparticle (GQD-NP) for targeted breast cancer therapy, which was sensitive to the physiological concentration of glutathione (GSH) within tumor tissues for the controlled release of doxorubicin (DOX). By encapsulating PEG and herceptin, GQD-NP conjugates also enhanced the half-life and cellular uptake of DOX in human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2)-overexpressing cancer [17].

There is an additional factor which could play an important role on drug encapsulation is the thermodynamic compatibility, for instance, the miscibility of the drug and nanoparticle-polymer complex system. Moreover, there are some thermodynamic parameters which are commonly used to assess drug nanoparticle-polymer miscibility such as solubility (δ) and the Flory-Huggins interaction parameters (χ). [18-22] In the rapidly developing field of nanomedicine, performing the optimal solubility of active component (anticancer drugs) and nanoparticle-polymer complex system presents an important study topic for targeted delivery of anticancer therapeutics, tumor imaging, or genes. Also, the thermodynamic compatibility between polymers and anticancer drugs indicates an important parameter, which determines the encapsulation efficiency (EE) of nanoparticle drug delivery systems. Stability of the formulation, drug loading, and also retention could be further improved via the optimal adjustment of polymer structures and compositions. [23]

Apart from stabilizing NPs in the blood circulation, determining how to increase cellular uptakes of NPs within tumor lesions is of major concern with regard to addressing cancer heterogeneity and adaptation [24,25]. The enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect of NPs is known for the accumulation of nanoscale particles passively captured in wider inter-endothelial gaps of tumor lesions [26]. However, recent work from Chan’s group indicated that about 97% of NPs enter tumor lesions via an active process, such as transcytosis instead of directly utilizing the EPR effect [27]. Due to the importance of active consumption by tumor cells, several strategies for enhancing cellular uptake can be adopted to fit this scope. Xia et al. designed polycationic polymer-coated silica NPs (MSNP-PEI) with non-covalent interactions, which employ polyethyleneimine (PEI) to ferry DNA or small interfering (si)RNA into the cytoplasm. The MSNP-PEI not only increases the cellular uptake of NPs but also minimizes the cytotoxicity of the cationic PEI [28]. Therefore, equipping NPs with cationic biopolymers is a judicious approach to raise cell uptake rates of NPs and further enhance the tumor-inhibitory efficacy.

Inspired by those works, our goal was to develop a functional nanocarrier with enhanced cellular uptake, pH-triggered features, and high biocompatibility, which could further improve the therapeutic effectiveness in cancer chemotherapy. Star-like GQD nanoclusters, denoted as GECDs, were synthesized by modifying GQDs through a two-step cross-linking method. CHO-PEG-NH2 was first linked with GQDs through carbodiimide chemistry to enhance the biocompatibility. Then, the terminal aldehyde groups on GQD-PEG were subsequently interacted with PEI through a Schiff base. Last, DOX was encapsulated by pi-interactions to form GECD nanoclusters. Moreover, the tumor microenvironment (TME) is known for its acidic nature, which can selectively alter the properties of nanocarriers. As for GECDs, the multiple amine groups can be protonated under low pH values after which they displayed higher positivity than in a physiological environment (pH 7.4). Protonated GECDs, with a high surface charge density, can therefore ferry an anticancer drug into the cytoplasm of cancer tissues through the proton-sponge effect owing to the potent electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cell membranes [29]. Subsequently, this acidic milieu prompts DOX to disengage from the GECDs. Both methods highly promote the efficacy of cancer inhibition and cancer targeting. Finally, functional assays in human cancer cells and in vivo mice xenograft experiments of GECD nanocarriers were performed to evaluate their antitumor potential.

Carboxylated GQDs was commercially obtained through Nanjing XFNANO materials Tech Co. Ltd. CHO-PEG-NH2 and polyethylenimine (PEI) (branched, M.W. ∼ 25,000) polymers were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. MES buffer, (1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride) (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were also purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Doxorubicin (hydrochloride) and Calcein AM solution were purchased from Cayman Chemical. PlasmocinTM treatment was purchased from InvivoGen. RPMI Medium, Antibiotic-Antimycotic, Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and Trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from Genetex.

To study the size and morphology of GECD, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7100, Japan) was carried out. UV–vis spectra were tested by Hitachi U-5100. The zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) spectra were examined by zetasizer (Nano-ZS). Photoluminescent spectra was implemented by JASCO FP-8300.

For starters, 3 mg of GQDs powder was dispersed in 3 mL deionized water through ultrasonic agitation in a bath sonicator (110 W) for half hour. The freshly prepared MES buffer was dosed in the GQD solution under mildly stirring. Subsequently, EDC (0.1 mL, 10.5 mM) and NHS (0.1 mL, 8.5 mM) were added dropwise and followed by the addition of NH2-PEG-CHO (3000 mw, 20 mg), and denoted as GE. The mixture was continuously stirred for overnight and then purified in dialysis tube (3.5–5 kDa) through ultrapure water. The GEC was synthesized by reacting with above mixture with 1 mg PEI and 10 μL 1 M NaOH via Schiff’s base reaction, then dialysis for overnight. Afterward, 1 mL as-prepared sample was mixed with Doxorubicin (1 mL, 1.8 mM) and sonicated for another 30 min. The reactant was repeated the same purification as aforementioned method and denoted as GECD for further examination.

To evaluate the in vitro drug release of GECD nanoparticles, dialysis method was implemented in a simulated physiological condition. An aliquot of GECD solution was packaged in dialysis bag (5 kDa) and immersed in two separate PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4 and pH 5.5) as experimental and control groups. The pH value was adjusted by 1 N HCl. At each time interval, 1 mL of GECD solution was taken out from the dialysis tube and then was determined based on absorbance at 480 nm with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

HCT116 human colon cancer cell lines (American Type Culture Collection) were cultured in RPMI medium (Gibco) containing 5–10% Fetal Bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1% Antibiotic-antimycotic (Gibco) in 37 °C, 5% CO2 incubator [30,31].

Colon cancer 4000–6000 cells/well were seeded in 96 well plates for overnight, then, cells were treated with DOX or GECD for indicated period of time, and then analyzed by MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay according to manufacturer's protocol (Goldbio) [32-34]. The readings of purple formazan crystals were determined by optical density at 570 nm by Epoch Microplate Spectrophotometer. The morphology of cells was inspected by the IX73 Inverted Microscope. Pictures of cell morphology with different concentrations of drug feeding were taken under the white light and fluorescence light (100 × ) of magnified fields.

This animal study was conducted under the guideline of the Animal Care and Use Committee of Taipei Medical University. 6 weeks old nude mice were applied in the xenograft experiment. 1 × 106 HCT116 cells were subcutaneous injected into the mice at right leg site, and the mice were grouped randomly. N ≥ 3 in each group. The body weight of the mice and the tumor size were measured 3 times a week. When the tumor size reached 100 mm3, on the day 12, 1.5 mg/kg DOX or 100 μL 10-fold GECD dilutions were applied to the nude mice 3 times a week by intraperitoneal injection. The treatments lasted for four weeks. At the end of the experiment, the mice were sacrificed, and the tumors were collected and measured [15,32]. Mean ± SEM was applied for body weight and tumor size analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the GEC-DOX system
ce:section.ce:para: The process by which GECDs were synthesized is illustrated in Schematic diagram 1 . Two kinds of biopolymers, PEG and PEI, were covalently linked to a GQD platform to enhance the biocompatibility, target cancer lesions, and prolong the blood circulation time of the nanocarrier. First, carboxylated GQDs were attached to amine-functionalized NH2-PEG-CHO through carbodiimide chemistry via its inherent –COOH functional groups, and these were denoted GE. Then, PEI was subsequently mixed with the former solution to connect the GQDs-PEG clusters through the addition of a Schiff base, denoted GEC. Finally, GECDs, with a star-like shape, were constructed with encapsulation of DOX through pi-interactions between the abundant aromatic rings on the surface of the GQDs.

The elemental configurations of GQDs, GE, GEC, and GECDs were examined by x-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) to verify the successful synthesis of the nanocarrier. As shown in Fig. S1, the survey displayed the entire spectrum containing C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s orbitals in GQDs, GE, GEC, and GECDs. To further investigate the cross-linking process and functional groups, high-resolution XPS were also implemented. Compared to GQDs, GE showed similar levels of O 1 s intensity due to the abundant C-C-O units within PEG (Fig. S2), whereas GEC had a smaller intensity due to the grafting of PEI onto GE which substituted oxygen atoms. The C 1 s spectrum of GQDs showed the obvious –COOH peak at 289.3 eV; however, there was no intensity in GE, suggesting the successful cross-linking of GQDs and NH2-PEG-CHO through carbodiimide chemistry (Fig. 1 a). To further examine the existence of Schiff bases, the N 1 s spectrum is displayed in Fig. 1b. The peak at 399.3 eV evidenced the existence of -N = bonds, indicating the presence of Schiff bases in GEC NPs. Also, because of the copious amine groups, GEC demonstrated notable N 1 s intensity, while GQDs had no intensity, and a small signal was observed in GE (Fig. 1b). The results suggested the sequential synthesis of GEC nanoclusters. Because of the similar elemental compositions of DOX and GEC, the existence of the anticancer drug in GECDs was examined with an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. For the comparison, the GECD sample was diluted four times to allow clear observation of differences between GEC and GECD. As shown in Fig. 3d, the broad shoulder at around 480 nm was assigned to the characteristic peak of DOX. This peak still existed after GEC was conjugated with DOX and then purified with a dialysis bag, suggesting the successful encapsulation of the anticancer drug.

After realizing the functional moieties of the nanocarrier, the surface charge behaviors were studied by the zeta potential and a DLS sizer. A series of modifications was also verified by a Zetasizer. As shown in Fig. 2 a, GQDs displayed a slightly negative value at about −4.77 mV. After introducing PEG through carbodiimide chemistry, the zeta potential increased to nearly neutral at around 1.79 mV. Then, the attachment of PEI by Schiff bases onto the PEG increased the zeta potential to 6.74 mV, whereas encapsulation of DOX into GEC slightly decreased the potential to about 4.43 mV. As shown in Table S1, before GE was attached to PEI, the size was about 1 μm, which was because of the swelling property of PEG molecules [35]. Then, the particle size shrank by interacting with PEI through Schiff bases. Throughout the synthesis procedure, the zeta potential of the NPs went from slightly negative to neutral and then mildly positive, which would favor uptake by cancer cells during chemotherapy treatment.

Owing to the aromatic structure of GQDs, their photoluminescent (PL) nature was investigated for the existence of GQDs. The PL spectrum with excitation at 325 nm was demonstrated to examine the optical properties throughout the cross-linking process. In Fig. 2b, GQDs displayed a broad peak from 350 to 500 nm, which indicated the intrinsic PL of GQDs. When GQDs were cross-linked with amine-functionalized PEG, the PL intensity was amplified. PEGylation was reported to increase the PL intensity of GQDs via covalent linkages, which also can be utilized to confirm the successful cross-linking between PEG and GQDs [36]. Furthermore, PEI was incorporated with GE through Schiff bases and still showed the characteristic PL peak of GQDs, indicating the integrity of the aromatic structures.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of GECDs are displayed in Fig. 3 a. GECD NPs displayed an anisotropic star-like shape with a uniform distribution. As shown in Fig. 3b, the high-resolution (HR)-TEM image shows that each size of the NPs was around 70 ∼ 80 nm, which coordinates with the size distribution (Fig. 3c). The star-like morphology is proposed to be the conjugation among clusters through Schiff base linking.

The drug encapsulation efficiency (EE%) and loading efficiency (LE%) of GECD nanocarriers were calculated by the absorption of DOX in UV–Vis spectra and the dry weight of the nanocarriers. The formulae are described as follows:
Encapsulation
E f f i c i e n c y
E E %
=
W
total
d r u g
-
W
free
d r u g
W
total
d r u g
× 100 %
Loading
E f f i c i e n c y
L E %
=
W
total
d r u g
-
W
free
d r u g
W
GECD
n a n o c a r r i e r
× 100 %

The EE and LE were calculated to be 51.52% and 32.66%, respectively. The in vitro drug release behavior of the GECD nanocarriers was investigated by immersing a drug-containing dialysis tube in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to simulate a physiological environment (Fig. 4 ). The pH-triggered nature of the drug carrier was examined by separate incubation in pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 PBS buffers. When the dialysis tube was immersed in pH 7.4 PBS, the DOX release percentage of the GECD solution showed a rapid change the first day, and then reached a plateau at around 45.5% for the next 3 days. In contrast, the drug release percentage of GECD NPs drastically increased to 60% in the pH 5.5 PBS solution in the first 24 h and then steadily released about 80% over the next 56 h. The expedited release phenomenon in pH 5.5 PBS was due to the protonation of amine groups of both DOX and GEC, resulting in the electrostatic repulsion which facilitated further drug release. The result demonstrates that only a tiny amount of DOX would be released under the simulated physiological conditions (pH 7.4); therefore, the delivery system indicated significant variation compared to the TME (pH 5.5). As a result, it could be concluded that GECD NPs showed different pH-triggered drug-release behaviors in pH 7.4 PBS and pH 5.5 PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell uptake evaluation of NPs in a transfection test
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate cellular uptake of NPs, we employed a green fluorescent protein (GFP) as an imaging agent to track the NPs under observation by fluorescence microscopy. The experiment was implemented by incubating GEC-GFP conjugates in HCT116 cells for various time points (24, 48, and 72 h). After the GEC-GFP conjugates were taken up by cancer cells, the green fluorescent light suggested that the nanocarriers had been transported to the cytoplasm. As shown in Fig. 5 , the control group showed no microscopic fluorescent signals even after 72 h, whereas the GEC group displayed a high number of green fluorescent cells, indicating enhanced cellular uptake. This result showed that the positively charged GEC NPs could enter the cytoplasm though an active pathway, which would favor the efficacy of anticancer treatment [37,38]. Another reason for the high cell uptake rate was the larger aspect ratio due to the star-like morphology of the GECDs, which facilitated the high cell uptake rate [39].

The cancer inhibition efficiency was investigated through an MTT assay at different time points with concentrations of an anticancer drug and composites (Fig. 6 ). After HCT116 cells were treated with GECDs with different DOX dosages (1, 3.5, and 11 μM), cell viability was analyzed after 24, 48, and 72 h. Moreover, 1, 5, and 10 μM DOX were employed as positive controls. Results showed that GECDs displayed excellent cancer inhibition in all three groups. Fluorescence microscopy was also applied to monitor the DOX intensity (red fluorescence), which was proportional to the concentration of DOX contained in each group. At 24 h, red fluorescence in the 3.5 μM GECD group showed a slightly lower intensity than that of the 5 μM DOX group, whereas the 3 μM GECD group displayed almost the same or an even higher intensity compared to the DOX group at 48 and 72 h. The results suggested that GECD NPs displayed a slow-release property after incubation with HCT116 cells for over 48 h. Moreover, the 1 μM GECD group exhibited the highest cytotoxicity after treatment for 48 h, which also suggested that our GECD nanocarriers exhibited powerful cancer inhibition efficiency at lower DOX concentrations.

A mouse xenograft experiment was carried out to investigate the tumor-inhibition capacity of GECD NPs as described in “Materials and methods”. When the tumor size reached 100 mm3 after an HCT116 subcutaneous injection, HCT116 (untreated group), DOX, or GECD were employed to evaluate the tumor-suppressive ability in a nude mice model. The tumor size and body weight of mice were monitored and measured throughout the experiment. Compared to the HCT116 (untreated) group, moderately restricted tumor growth was achieved in the DOX group with about 40% tumor suppression (Fig. 7 a). Such efficacy was significantly higher in the GECD group, possibly due to the synergistic effect of high cell uptake and the pH-triggered release property. Treatment in the GECD group resulted in dramatic cancer inhibition efficiency of about 82%, which was nearly double that of the DOX group (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. 7c, body weights of mice in the three groups showed similar trends, which indicated that none of the three treatments had any obvious toxicity. At the end of the in vivo experiment, the mice were sacrificed, and the tumors were collected as shown in the Fig. 7d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion about a possible cell-entry mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we developed a novel drug carrier system that has not been described before, and demonstrated its cancer suppression potential in vitro and in vivo. Even though the evidence is still limited and further experiments are in need in the future, we propose one of the possible cell-entry mechanisms for our drug delivery system. GECD nanocarriers possess a neutral charge in a physiological environment of pH 7.4. After entering the TME, the inherent acidity of the tumor milieu could protonate the GECDs through free terminal amines, resulting in higher positivity that can easily interact with negatively charged cell membrane. This process can then activate the proton-sponge effect and active transport of nanocarriers, to facilitate the delivery of anticancer drugs [40-42]. Schematic diagram 2 illustrates the possible mechanism of our drug delivery system [43,44].

Star-like GQD nanoclusters (GECDs) were successfully synthesized and can potentially be used as a drug carrier for colon cancer inhibition. The XPS analyses indicated the successful cross-linking of the two biopolymers. UV–Vis spectra verified the non-covalent encapsulation of the anticancer drug (DOX). The zeta potential results showed the transformation from a near neutral value at pH 7 to mildly positive at pH 5, suggesting the potential variation between normal cells and cancer cells for effective targeting.

Compared to the DOX and control groups, GECDs displayed the potent capability to suppress cancer cell proliferation and growth of tumors in vivo with no obvious toxic effects. The results suggest that cationic polymers and an anisotropic shape can facilitate cell uptake, which highly enhanced the suppression of colon cancer.

Guang-Yu Lee: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Pei-Ying Lo: Investigation. Er-Chieh Cho: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Jia-Huei Zheng: Methodology. Min Li: Methodology. Jen-Hsien Huang: Formal analysis. Kuen-Chan Lee: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was partly supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST 109-2320-B-038-038, MOST 110-2314-B-152-001 and MOST 110-2320-B-038-027), Taiwan, and WanFang Hospital, Chi-Mei Medical Center, and Hualien Tzu-Chi Hospital Joint Cancer Center Grant-Focus on Colon Cancer Research (MOHW110-TDU-B-212-144020, funded by Health and welfare surcharge of tobacco products), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University and the CPC Corporation, Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2021.100320.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

N. Engl. J. Med.
